dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain at [CITY].
Hi, my name is Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what brought you to call 911 today, sir?

Hello, come in here. The patient is my coworker, he's from the kitchen, and he started saying he was having chest pressure during work around noon. Is it okay if we check him out quickly in the utility closet where he is now?
Sir, can you describe the chest pain you're experiencing?
It started suddenly around noon today. I was working with the fryer, and I felt a sharp pain, like someone had hit me in the chest with a big hammer.
The pain fluctuates-it's sometimes a 4, other times it jumps up to 9. It cycles roughly every 4 minutes.
Does the pain go anywhere else in your body, like your arm, back, jaw, or neck?
No, it just stays right here in my chest.
On a scale of 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst pain you've ever had in your life, how would you rate your chest pain now?
Right now, it's a 9. Sometimes it drops back down to a 4, but it still really stings when it comes back.
How long does the pain last before it eases, and how long until it returns?
Each episode lasts for just a few seconds, and it comes back in about 4 minutes.
Can you tell me your name?
My name is Dave.
Dave, can you tell me where we are now?
I think we're in a restaurant kitchen in [CITY].
Dave, can you tell me the date and time now?
I'm not 100% sure of the time, but it has to be a little after 2 in the afternoon.
You're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event.
Your motor response is appropriate; you're able to follow verbal commands and move consistently.
You're eye opening is normal and spontaneous.
Your verbal responses are coherent and appropriate to the questions asked.
Your airway is patent and self-maintained.
I see the trachea is midline with no signs of obstruction.
Your breathing appears to be shallow, but your rhythm is consistent.
How is your respiratory effort right now?
That's right. I can catch my breath, but I often have to take a deep breath in between sentences.
Let me count your breaths. They appear to be at a rate of 18 per minute and shallow.
Are you okay with us taking your blood pressure now?
I need to check your pulse at the wrist. It is strong and regular, which is a good sign.
We'll now check your blood oxygen with a clip on your finger and do a quick finger prick for your blood glucose level.
Your oxygen saturation is 96% and your blood glucose measured at 107 mg/dL.
Dave, we are going to do an EKG to look at your heart rhythm. I'll need to place 12 leads. Okay with you?
I'm going to place an IV in your left wrist in case we need to give you fluids or medication. You may feel slight pressure as I do so.
Okay, go ahead. I trust you.
Your skin is warm, pink, and dry. Just covered in flour from work.
Are you free of any neck pain or discomfort?
Yes, I don't feel any pressure or pain in the neck at all.
Do you have any medical conditions or history we should know about?
I have mild asthma-nothing severe, just occasional use of an inhaler.
Let me recheck your blood pressure now.
Your pulse is now at 92 and regular. Your oxygen level is at 100 now and trending up.
Your blood pressure is down to 115 over 76. Vitals are trending in the right direction.
The EKG shows normal findings-regular sinus rhythm with occasional ventricular premature complexes. No acute changes identified.
Based on the findings, we'll transport you under a chest pain protocol for further evaluation at the hospital.
The nearest hospital is [COMPANY_NAME]. Is that okay for you?
Yeah, [CITY] is good. I've been there before in emergencies.
Dave, I need you to stand up slowly and pivot to the cot with help from me and my partner.
I'm ready to move. Just take it slow and easy.
I've raised the rails along both sides of the cot for safety.
Once you're on the cot, we'll secure you with straps for transport.
I'm giving you a dose of aspirin now as per protocol for chest pain. We'll also be running normal saline via IV.
I'm placing you in a full Fowler's position for comfort and airway maintenance.
Your GCS is 15 with eye opening at 4, verbal response at 5, and motor response at 6. You are fully alert and oriented.
Your position is the one you're most comfortable in.
I'll be monitoring your vitals via the EKG and emergency monitoring equipment throughout transport.
I'm going to call [COMPANY_NAME] now to prepare for your arrival and pass along your key vitals.
Your condition is stable, so we'll proceed with a standard transport pace without lights or sirens.
I'll log the aspirin dose time and IV insertion as well.
Alright, let's help Dave onto the cot. Slowly, we've got you.
He's on the cot now. Ready to be secured.
Rail's up and strapped in. You good back there, Dave?
Yep, good. No problems here.
You are now secured with five-point straps and rails in place. We are ready to move.
Dave, we'll monitor you continuously during the ride to the hospital.
We are all set and ready to transport you to the hospital. Let's get started.
Initial vitals at 14:38 were pulse 86, respiratory rate 18, blood pressure 130/82, SpO₂ 96, glucose 107.
Retake vitals at 14:47 were pulse 92, respiratory rate 18, blood pressure 115/76, SpO₂ 100.
Dave is conscious and comfortable. Let's keep monitoring his vitals on the way.
Vital signs are stable. No acute distress noticed.
Just relax, Dave. We'll make it to the hospital safely.
Vitals are still good. Pulse is 92, O2 at 100. Nothing concerning for now.
We're pulling up now to the emergency department. A nurse is on standby to receive you.
Dave, I'm about to help you out of the ambulance and into the hospital wheelchair.
Once there, let the nurse know we gave your report, and they'll get you to triage.
We have already given you aspirin and the IV is running fine. Any contraindications?
No, I don't have any concerns about that.
I got the EKG on file and will hand it over at the hospital.
I'm calling the hospital in now for a complete patient report summary.
Dave, I'll help you over to the wheelchair once the ambulance is parked.
Just make sure to let the triage nurse know we gave you the report and when you arrive.
Final vitals on the way to the hospital-pulse is 92, respirations 18, SpO₂ still at 100, blood pressure 115/76.
Aspirin was administered and IV remains running fine. All set for transfer.
Just a quick stop before we hand you over-will you sign off on the transport paperwork?
Yes, I'll sign now.
We are in front of the hospital. Nurse is ready for the report package.
I'll now transfer care to the wheelchair and assist you as the nurse takes it from there.
We've given the nurse a full report and passed along all the documentation.
Transportation complete and patient has been handed off safely to the hospital for further care.
